summary: Add `--config CONFIG` support to CloudSploit so a JS credentials file can replace per-call env-var credentials. Add a `config` parameter to `CloudSploitToolkit.run_scan` / `run_compliance_scan` (and a `config_file` field on `CloudSploitConfig` for an instance-wide default), wire it through `CloudSploitExecutor` into `_build_cli_args` (emitting `--config=<path>`), and — when running under Docker — read-only-mount the host file into the container so the in-container `--config` path resolves.

localization.role: add optional `config_file: Optional[str]` field (mirrors the `gcp_credentials_path` precedent)
accept a `config_path` kw-arg and prepend `--config=<path>` when set
thread a `config: Optional[str]` parameter through and, when set, also pass a `(host_dir, container_dir)` mount for the config file's parent dir
widen `volume_mount` from a single tuple to a list of tuples so we can mount BOTH output dir AND config dir simultaneously
expose a `config: Optional[str] = None` agent-tool argument that falls back to `self.config.config_file`
argv-level assertions for: config arg → --config in argv; default-from-config-model; volume mount appears; ro flag; no flag emitted when config is None

constraints.claim: The CLI accepts `--config=<path>` and treats the path as a JS module (e.g., `config.js`), with provider-specific exports. Inline env-var credentials become inert when --config is present.
Under Docker the host config-file path must be mounted into the container; only the in-container path is valid as the `--config` value.
Credentials files are sensitive — the mount must be read-only (`:ro`) to prevent accidental writes by the container.
The existing `volume_mount: Optional[tuple[str, str]]` slot supports only one mount; mounting both an output dir AND a config dir requires widening the type to a list.
When `config` is None on a call AND `CloudSploitConfig.config_file` is None, behaviour is unchanged (env-var credentials, no `--config` arg).

recent_history.note: CloudSploit toolkit was recently migrated from packages/ai-parrot to packages/ai-parrot-tools (the templates/ rebuild this morning is part of the same migration). No churn on executor.py beyond that.

hypothesis.statement: Add a single `config: Optional[str]` parameter to the toolkit methods and a `config_file` default in CloudSploitConfig. The executor splits responsibilities: _build_cli_args emits `--config=<container_path>`; _run_with_outputs orchestrates the mount (host_dir read-only, in-container path passed to args). Falls back cleanly when neither call-time nor config-time value is set.

in_scope: New `config_file` field on CloudSploitConfig (Optional[str], default None).
New `config` parameter on toolkit.run_scan and toolkit.run_compliance_scan.
Read-only volume mount for the host config file.
Unit tests covering argv shape, default fallback, mount presence, and no-config baseline.
Docstring updates explaining when env-var creds vs --config takes effect.
out_of_scope: Validating/parsing the JS config file's contents (CloudSploit does that).
Generating a config file from CloudSploitConfig credentials — keep the two paths orthogonal.
Multi-config-file support (one file per call is enough).

confidence_map.claim: `--config` is the right CLI flag (not a synonym).
Config file is a JS module, not JSON.
Mount must be read-only for safety.
Single-tuple volume_mount slot is the only blocker on the Docker side.
No existing callers of the executor rely on volume_mount being a single tuple (only internal callsite is _run_with_outputs).
Existing tests can be extended without spinning up Docker.

unknowns.question: What is the in-container mount path convention? Options: (a) `/cloudsploit/config/<basename>` (mirrors _DOCKER_OUTPUT_MOUNT); (b) `/cloudsploit/etc/<basename>`; (c) accept the host's absolute path and reuse it inside the container.

Per-call `config` argument behaviour when *both* `run_scan(config=...)` and `CloudSploitConfig.config_file` are set — does the call-level value override (the natural Python default), or do we raise an error to surface conflicts?

recommended_next_command.rationale: Localization is high-confidence (5 cited files, all in one small module). Constraints are well-grounded in the upstream docs. The two unknowns are narrow API-shape choices the spec author can decide in <5 minutes.
